text:
Enterprise AI platform
for healthcare operations
textPreview: Enterprise AI platform | for healthcare operations

text: A synthetic-data-only demonstration of MLOps, LLMOps, governance, and Azure deployment.
textPreview: A synthetic-data-only demonstration of MLOps, LLMOps, governance, and Azure deployment.

text: Safe rollout  ·  observable services  ·  repeatable platform reuse
textPreview: Safe rollout  ·  observable services  ·  repeatable platform reuse

text:
This is CareAI Platform, a production-style demo of how I would design an AI platform for payer and healthcare-operations use cases without real PHI. The point is not one model or one chatbot; it is a reusable platform for data, models, prompts, deployments, governance, monitoring, and rollback.

My interview framing: it helps operations teams move from isolated AI experiments to governed, deployable, observable AI services.
The product: governed AI services for operations teams
textPreview: The product: governed AI services for operations teams

text:
Payment integrity
Claims operations
Prior authorization
Member support
Internal AI platform teams
textPreview: Payment integrity | Claims operations | Prior authorization | Member support | Internal AI platform teams

text:
Asset registry
Reusable inference and RAG APIs
Approval workflows
Auditability and lineage
textPreview: Asset registry | Reusable inference and RAG APIs | Approval workflows | Auditability and lineage

text:
Quality and drift
Latency and reliability
Groundedness and safety
Safe rollout and rollback
textPreview: Quality and drift | Latency and reliability | Groundedness and safety | Safe rollout and rollback

text: One platform, two execution patterns: predictive ML + document-grounded GenAI.
textPreview: One platform, two execution patterns: predictive ML + document-grounded GenAI.

text:
CareAI is an internal AI platform for healthcare operations teams that need secure, governed, reusable AI services across predictive models and document-reasoning workflows.

Direct users are platform engineers, ML engineers, data scientists, and operations stakeholders. The business-facing customers are teams that need faster decisions, consistent workflows, and clear operational visibility.
The control plane is the center of gravity
textPreview: The control plane is the center of gravity

text:
Datasets · models · prompts
Deployments · approvals
Evaluation · audit · workflows
textPreview: Datasets · models · prompts | Deployments · approvals | Evaluation · audit · workflows

text: Validated claims-risk scoring, model metadata, monitoring and fallback behavior
textPreview: Validated claims-risk scoring, model metadata, monitoring and fallback behavior

text: Role-filtered retrieval, approved prompts, cited answers and safety checks
textPreview: Role-filtered retrieval, approved prompts, cited answers and safety checks

text:
This is the 30-second system view. The control plane tracks datasets, model artifacts, prompt templates, approvals, deployments, audit events, evaluation runs, and bounded workflow state. Two serving planes execute the work: a prediction API for claims-risk inference and a RAG gateway for policy and operations Q&A. Pipelines feed both sides, and monitoring closes the loop.

The design is local-first for development, but Azure-native for deployment.
Governance is built into the lifecycle—not bolted on
textPreview: Governance is built into the lifecycle—not bolted on

text:
Datasets
Models and deployments
Prompt templates
Evaluation runs
Model cards and prompt cards
textPreview: Datasets | Models and deployments | Prompt templates | Evaluation runs | Model cards and prompt cards

text:
Model card + approval before production

Prompt card + approval before production

Stage transitions are policy controlled
textPreview: Model card + approval before production |  | Prompt card + approval before production |  | Stage transitions are policy controlled

text:
Correlation IDs on mutations

Audit events

Lineage to data, metrics, code and run IDs
textPreview: Correlation IDs on mutations |  | Audit events |  | Lineage to data, metrics, code and run IDs

text: The platform knows what changed, who approved it, and why it is deployed.
textPreview: The platform knows what changed, who approved it, and why it is deployed.

text:
I position the control plane as the governance and workflow brain. It stores metadata, lineage, evaluation status, approval decisions, and rollout state. This is where platform maturity shows up: it is not just serving predictions; it knows what is deployed, why it was approved, and what changed.

The key point is that production promotion is policy-controlled, not an ad hoc API call.
MLOps: train, govern, deploy, observe, improve
textPreview: MLOps: train, govern, deploy, observe, improve

text: The deployment decision is governed; the rollback path is explicit.
textPreview: The deployment decision is governed; the rollback path is explicit.

text:
The traditional ML flow starts with synthetic claims-like data, trains a risk model, logs runs to MLflow, then writes metadata to the control plane. That gives both experiment tracking and enterprise metadata. Approvals and model cards then gate deployment.

The lifecycle is concrete: dev, candidate, staging, approved, production, and deprecated.
Inference is designed to fail safely
textPreview: Inference is designed to fail safely

text:
Validate the request schema
Load the active artifact
Return model metadata with the score
Emit prediction and audit events
Record latency and monitoring signals
textPreview: Validate the request schema | Load the active artifact | Return model metadata with the score | Emit prediction and audit events | Record...

text:
Champion / challenger routing
Canary traffic split
Rollback target in deployment metadata
Rollback recommendation from latency, error, and drift signals
Deterministic rules fallback if model is unavailable
textPreview: Champion / challenger routing | Canary traffic split | Rollback target in deployment metadata | Rollback recommendation from latency, err...

text: Live local finding: the service is healthy, but currently in explicit rules-fallback mode because no model URI is configured.
textPreview: Live local finding: the service is healthy, but currently in explicit rules-fallback mode because no model URI is configured.

text:
The inference service does not blindly assume the model is present and healthy. It validates inputs, has freshness and missingness checks, returns explicit model metadata, and uses deterministic rules if the artifact is unavailable.

For rollout safety, champion/challenger routing and traffic splits are supported. In a production environment, the same control pattern can front a traffic router or service mesh. In the current local stack, the endpoint reports fallback mode because no model URI is mounted into the container.
LLMOps: grounded answers with retrieval, safety, and audit
textPreview: LLMOps: grounded answers with retrieval, safety, and audit

text: Azure AI Search is the cloud retrieval path; a local JSON vector index keeps the interview demo self-contained.
textPreview: Azure AI Search is the cloud retrieval path; a local JSON vector index keeps the interview demo self-contained.

text:
Documents are chunked, embedded, and indexed into Azure AI Search or a local fallback index. At query time, the service enforces role-based retrieval filters, selects an approved prompt template when available, runs safety checks, and requires citations.

The important design choice is groundedness and traceability instead of a free-form answer engine.
The platform is organized around bounded operational loops
textPreview: The platform is organized around bounded operational loops

text:
Retrieve context
Answer with citations
Verify groundedness
Retry once if needed
textPreview: Retrieve context | Answer with citations | Verify groundedness | Retry once if needed

text:
Plan one allowlisted tool
Execute it
Verify evidence
Retry policy retrieval once
Hand off failures
textPreview: Plan one allowlisted tool | Execute it | Verify evidence | Retry policy retrieval once | Hand off failures

text:
Request approval
Gate promotion
Deploy safely
Monitor results
Rollback or retrain
textPreview: Request approval | Gate promotion | Deploy safely | Monitor results | Rollback or retrain

text:
Capture prediction events
Run drift checks
Record eval runs
Audit change history
Trigger review
textPreview: Capture prediction events | Run drift checks | Record eval runs | Audit change history | Trigger review

text: A reusable platform is a set of durable feedback loops—not a one-shot model invocation.
textPreview: A reusable platform is a set of durable feedback loops—not a one-shot model invocation.

text:
If asked whether this follows loop engineering, the answer is yes. The RAG system has a retrieval and validation loop. MLOps has a deployment and monitoring loop. Governance has an approval loop.

The control plane also has a cross-service case loop: it deterministically plans one allowlisted tool, executes it, verifies evidence, retries incomplete policy retrieval once, and creates a human-review item for other failures. WorkflowRun planner_state_json keeps the safe plan, verification, retry, and handoff history. This is custom bounded orchestration today, not LangGraph or an unbounded LLM agent. The next cloud step is a durable queue or Container Apps Job scheduler.
Observability closes the gap between deployment and trust
textPreview: Observability closes the gap between deployment and trust

text:
Prediction count
Latency and error rate
Risk-band mix
Feature missingness
Drift against baseline
textPreview: Prediction count | Latency and error rate | Risk-band mix | Feature missingness | Drift against baseline

text:
Evaluation metrics
Groundedness
Citation coverage
Safety-flag counts
Prompt version traceability
textPreview: Evaluation metrics | Groundedness | Citation coverage | Safety-flag counts | Prompt version traceability

text:
Correlated structured logs
OpenTelemetry traces
Application Insights
Event Hubs publishing
Audit events
textPreview: Correlated structured logs | OpenTelemetry traces | Application Insights | Event Hubs publishing | Audit events

text: Simplified drift logic today; correct operational seams for a production-grade monitor tomorrow.
textPreview: Simplified drift logic today; correct operational seams for a production-grade monitor tomorrow.

text:
Prediction events are persisted for monitoring. Drift checks compare recent serving distributions to training baselines. Logs and traces use a request correlation ID across services. Event Hubs support is present so the architecture can become more event-driven without changing service contracts.

Drift detection is intentionally lightweight today, but the operational seams are correct.
Azure deployment: Container Apps as the default path
textPreview: Azure deployment: Container Apps as the default path

text: Control plane · inference · RAG · web console
textPreview: Control plane · inference · RAG · web console

text:
The default cloud path is Azure Container Apps plus ACR, with Terraform managing the resource group, environment, storage, search, observability, and optional services like PostgreSQL, Redis, Event Hubs, and Azure ML. It shows cloud maturity without introducing Kubernetes complexity by default.

Managed identity is used for image pulls and Azure resource access; runtime secrets should be externalized through Key Vault references. The remaining operating seams are explicit: publish the trained model bundle to Blob Storage, run Azure AI Search ingestion, and add a Container Apps Job or queue worker for scheduled workflow execution.

textPreview: Why it still demonstrates production judgment

Known gaps are called out, not hidden
textPreview: Architecture is realistic | Control points are production-aligned | Azure deployment path is concrete | Governance and audit boundaries a...

text: Honesty about scope is an asset in a system-design interview.
textPreview: Honesty about scope is an asset in a system-design interview.

text:
I want to be direct about what is mocked. The point is not to pretend this is a live healthcare production platform. The point is to demonstrate system design judgment: governance boundaries, deployment patterns, lifecycle management, and operational feedback loops.

That honesty shows I understand the edges of a real production system.

A reusable AI platform—
not a collection of demos.
textPreview: A reusable AI platform— | not a collection of demos.

text: Predictive ML and RAG share governance and operations.
textPreview: Predictive ML and RAG share governance and operations.

text: Approval gates, safe rollout, observability, and rollback.
textPreview: Approval gates, safe rollout, observability, and rollback.

text: Built for complex workflows, human review, and traceability.
textPreview: Built for complex workflows, human review, and traceability.

text: Discussion paths: governance · MLOps rollout safety · RAG loops · Azure operations
textPreview: Discussion paths: governance · MLOps rollout safety · RAG loops · Azure operations

text:
My closing line: this design fits a payer-focused AI platform role because it brings together reusable services, healthcare-aware governance, deployment automation, and production observability. It supports complex operational workflows, not isolated demos.

If the panel wants to go deeper, I can branch into control plane governance, MLOps rollout safety, RAG and loop engineering, or Azure deployment and operations.
